section.title: The Letter to the Exiles
section.sectionRationale: Jeremiah sends a letter to the first wave of exiles already carried off to Babylon, overturning the false hope of a quick return. Instead of resistance or despair, God calls His people to settle down, build, and seek the peace of the very city that holds them captive, while still promising that after seventy years He will bring them home with a future and a hope.
section.thesis: God's word to the exiles is to live faithfully within Babylon rather than resist it, trusting a specific covenant promise of restoration after seventy years rather than the comforting lies of false prophets, so that verse 11's plans to prosper you is first of all a promise to the exiled nation as a whole, not a private guarantee of an easy life for any individual who claims it.
section.frameNote: Five scenes: instructions to settle in Babylon and seek the welfare of the city; the specific promise of restoration after seventy years, a future and a hope; judgment on the false prophets remaining in Jerusalem; the fiery death decreed for Ahab and Zedekiah who prophesied lies in Babylon; and the rebuke of Shemaiah for opposing Jeremiah's true word.

units.label: Settle down and seek the city's welfare

verses.text: This is the text of the letter that Jeremiah the prophet sent from Jerusalem to the surviving elders among the exiles and to the priests, the prophets, and all the others Nebuchadnezzar had carried into exile from Jerusalem to Babylon.
(This was after King Jeconiah, the queen mother, the court officials, the officials of Judah and Jerusalem, the craftsmen, and the metalsmiths had been exiled from Jerusalem.)
The letter was entrusted to Elasah son of Shaphan and Gemariah son of Hilkiah, whom Zedekiah king of Judah sent to King Nebuchadnezzar in Babylon. It stated:
This is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says to all the exiles who were carried away from Jerusalem to Babylon:
“Build houses and settle down. Plant gardens and eat their produce.
Take wives and have sons and daughters. Take wives for your sons and give your daughters in marriage, so that they too may have sons and daughters. Multiply there; do not decrease.
Seek the {{shalom|prosperity}} of the city to which I have sent you as exiles. Pray to the LORD on its behalf, for if it prospers, you too will prosper.”
For this is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: “Do not be deceived by the prophets and diviners among you, and do not listen to the dreams you elicit from them.
For they are falsely prophesying to you in My name; I have not sent them, declares the LORD.”

units.label: Seventy years, then a future and a hope
units.note: a specific promise to a specific exile

verses.text: For this is what the LORD says: “When Babylon's seventy years are complete, I will attend to you and confirm My promise to restore you to this place.
For I know the {{machashabah|plans}} I have for you, declares the LORD, plans to prosper you and not to harm you, to give you a future and a hope.
Then you will call upon Me and come and pray to Me, and I will listen to you.
You will seek Me and find Me when you search for Me with all your heart.

I will be found by you, declares the LORD, and I will {{shubshevut|restore}} you from captivity and gather you from all the nations and places to which I have banished you, declares the LORD. I will restore you to the place from which I sent you into exile.”

units.label: Judgment on the prophets left in Jerusalem

verses.text: Because you may say, “The LORD has raised up for us prophets in Babylon,”
this is what the LORD says about the king who sits on David's throne and all the people who remain in this city, your brothers who did not go with you into exile,

this is what the LORD of Hosts says: 
“I will send against them sword and famine and plague, and I will make them like rotten figs, so bad they cannot be eaten.

I will pursue them with sword and famine and plague. I will make them a horror to all the kingdoms of the earth, a curse, a desolation, and an object of scorn and reproach among all the nations to which I banish them.

I will do this because they have not listened to My words, declares the LORD, which I sent to them again and again through My servants the prophets. And neither have you exiles listened, declares the LORD.”

units.label: Ahab and Zedekiah's lying prophecy
units.note: a curse and a fiery death

verses.text: So hear the word of the LORD, all you exiles I have sent away from Jerusalem to Babylon.

This is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says about Ahab son of Kolaiah and Zedekiah son of Maaseiah, who are prophesying to you lies in My name: “I will deliver them to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, and he will kill them before your very eyes.
Because of them, all the exiles of Judah who are in Babylon will use this curse: ‘May the LORD make you like Zedekiah and Ahab, whom the king of Babylon roasted in the fire!’

For they have committed an outrage in Israel by committing adultery with the wives of their neighbors and speaking lies in My name, which I did not command them to do. I am He who knows, and I am a witness, declares the LORD.”

units.label: Shemaiah's opposition and judgment
units.note: a lie the exiles were made to trust

verses.text: You are to tell Shemaiah the Nehelamite that

this is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: “In your own name you have sent out letters to all the people of Jerusalem, to the priest Zephaniah son of Maaseiah, and to all the priests. You said to Zephaniah:

‘The LORD has appointed you priest in place of Jehoiada, to be the chief officer in the house of the LORD, responsible for any madman who acts like a prophet, you must put him in stocks and neck irons.
So now, why have you not rebuked Jeremiah of Anathoth, who poses as a prophet among you?
For he has sent to us in Babylon, claiming: Since the exile will be lengthy, build houses and settle down; plant gardens and eat their produce.’”
(Zephaniah the priest, however, had read this letter to Jeremiah the prophet.)
Then the word of the LORD came to Jeremiah:
“Send a message telling all the exiles what the LORD says concerning Shemaiah the Nehelamite. Because Shemaiah has prophesied to you, though I did not send him, and has made you trust in a lie,

this is what the LORD says: ‘I will surely punish Shemaiah the Nehelamite and his descendants. He will have no one left among this people, nor will he see the good that I will bring to My people, declares the LORD, for he has preached rebellion against the LORD.’”

shalom.gloss: Peace, welfare, wholeness, prosperity. Jeremiah tells the exiles to seek the shalom of Babylon itself and to pray for it, tying their own welfare to the welfare of the pagan city that holds them captive.

machashabah.gloss: A plan, thought, or purpose. God's machashabah for the exiles is fixed to the seventy-year timeframe just named in verse 10, a specific national promise of return, not a blank check for any individual reader's circumstances.

shubshevut.gloss: To restore from captivity, literally to turn or return a captivity. This recurring phrase across Jeremiah's later chapters, 30:3, 32:44, 33:7, names the specific covenant reversal God promises His exiled people.

basic.context: Jeremiah writes a letter from Jerusalem to the exiles already taken to Babylon in the first deportation. Rather than promising a quick return, as popular prophets were doing, he tells them to build houses, plant gardens, marry, and seek the good of the city that holds them, because God's actual plan is restoration only after seventy years.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real pastoral letter to a specific community in a specific crisis. Verse 11, plans to prosper you, is often quoted as a private promise, but in context it is God's plan for the whole exiled nation, tied to the seventy years of verse 10, not a guarantee of ease for any individual believer who claims it today.
basic.originalLanguages: God tells the exiles to seek the shalom, the welfare or peace, of Babylon itself. His machashabah, His plan, for them is a future and a hope, but it unfolds only after the shuv shevut, the reversal of their captivity, which He has already set at seventy years.
basic.typology: The exiles living as faithful sojourners in a pagan city, praying for its good while awaiting a promised homecoming, anticipates the New Testament picture of Christians as exiles and sojourners in the world, 1 Peter 2:11-12, seeking the good of the city while awaiting their true homeland.
basic.christ: The future and a hope promised here to national Israel finds its deepest fulfillment in Christ, in whom all of God's promises are yes and amen, 2 Corinthians 1:20. The pattern of God's good purposes for His exiled people, purposes for welfare and not for harm, is most fully secured for believers in the finished work of Jesus.
basic.god: God is sovereign even over the length of the exile, He names it at seventy years, and faithful to His covenant even while His people suffer its just consequences. He also confronts comforting lies spoken in His name with the same seriousness as any other sin, promising to hold false prophets accountable.
basic.crossReferences: Daniel 9:2 shows Daniel reading this very promise of seventy years and being moved to prayer. 1 Peter 2:11-12 calls Christians to the same posture as exiles seeking the good of a foreign city. Romans 8:28 echoes God's purpose for good even within hardship. Matthew 24:24 warns of false prophets exactly like those Jeremiah confronts here.
basic.oneStory: God's word to His exiled people is not escape from Babylon but faithfulness within it, grounded in a specific promise of restoration and a future hope. That pattern, real hardship held together with a certain hope in God's good purposes, culminates in Christ, in whom every promise of future and hope is secured for God's people.

context.literary: A pastoral letter embedded in the narrative, addressed to a named community in exile and confronting rival letters and rival prophets, making this one of the most direct, personal passages in the book.
context.historical: Written after the 597 BC deportation had already carried King Jeconiah, the queen mother, officials, and craftsmen to Babylon, while Zedekiah still reigned in Jerusalem and popular prophets on both sides of the exile promised an imminent return.
context.canonical: This chapter supplies the seventy-year timeframe that Daniel later reads and prays over, Daniel 9:2, anchoring the exile and return in a datable, covenant-bound promise rather than open-ended uncertainty.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To correct the false hope of a quick return by calling the exiles to settle into ordinary, faithful life in Babylon, while anchoring their hope in a specific, dated promise of restoration rather than the comforting lies of false prophets.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a specific letter to a specific exiled community with a specific timeframe; it does not prescribe seventy years for every hardship. It does establish the enduring pattern of faithful living within hard circumstances while trusting God's revealed, not invented, promises.

debates.question: Is Jeremiah 29:11 a promise of personal prosperity for any believer who claims it?

views.label: A corporate promise to exiled Israel alone
views.case: The you is plural throughout, addressed to the whole exile community, and the promise is explicitly bound to the seventy years of verse 10, a specific national restoration rather than a private guarantee of an easy or successful life.

views.label: A pattern that legitimately extends to God's people in Christ
views.case: While first spoken to Israel in exile, the verse reveals God's character, His good purposes for His covenant people, and Christians grafted into that covenant through Christ can rightly find confidence in the same God's good intentions, held with appropriate humility about timing and circumstance.

debates.bearing: Reading the verse in context guards against turning it into a private prosperity slogan, while still allowing real, Christ-secured hope in God's good purposes for His people.

study.originalLanguages: The exiles are told to seek the shalom, the welfare, of Babylon itself, a striking command to pray for the peace of their captors. God's machashabah, His plan, is fixed to the seventy years already named, and its fulfillment is the shuv shevut, the reversal of captivity, a phrase that recurs through Jeremiah's later chapters of hope.

typology.type: Exiles called to seek the welfare of a pagan city, Jeremiah 29:7
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the New Testament call to Christians as sojourners and exiles to seek the good of the city where they live, 1 Peter 2:11-12.

typology.type: A future and a hope promised after a set exile, Jeremiah 29:10-11
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the certain hope secured for believers in Christ, in whom every promise of God finds its yes, 2 Corinthians 1:20.

study.christ: The plans to prosper you of verse 11 are first a national promise of return from a datable exile, but the pattern they reveal, that God's purposes for His people are for welfare and a future, not harm, finds its full and certain guarantee in Christ. In Him every promise of God is yes and amen, 2 Corinthians 1:20, and believers, like the exiles, are called to seek the good of the place they now live while they wait for their true homecoming.
study.god: God is sovereign even over the exact length of the exile, naming it at seventy years, and He is faithful to bring His people home exactly as He said. Yet He takes no less seriously the sin of prophets, whether in Jerusalem or in Babylon, who speak comforting lies in His name, promising them the same accountability He brought on Hananiah.

crossReferences.note: Daniel reads Jeremiah's seventy-year promise and is moved to intense, repentant prayer for his people.
Calls Christians, like the exiles, to live as sojourners who seek the good of the place they now live.
Every promise of God, including the future and hope promised here, finds its yes and amen in Christ.
Warns of false prophets and false christs, the same danger Jeremiah confronts in Ahab, Zedekiah, and Shemaiah.

study.oneStory: God's letter to the exiles calls them to settle into faithful, ordinary life in Babylon rather than resist it, anchoring their hope not in comforting lies but in a specific, dated promise of restoration and a future. That same pattern, real hardship held together with certain hope in God's good purposes, comes to its fullest guarantee in Christ, in whom every promise of a future and a hope is secured for God's people.

quiz.q: What does Jeremiah's letter instruct the exiles to do in Babylon?

options.text: Build houses, plant gardens, marry, and seek the welfare of the city that holds them captive.
options.note: Verses 5 to 7 give these specific, ordinary instructions for settled life in exile.

options.text: Prepare for an armed uprising against Babylon.
options.note: The letter's message is the opposite, settle in and seek the city's peace, not rebellion.

options.text: Refuse to marry or have children until the return to Jerusalem.
options.note: The letter explicitly commands the opposite, take wives, have children, multiply there.

options.text: Wait passively without engaging in the life of the city at all.
options.note: The command goes further than waiting, it calls for active building, planting, and prayer for the city.

quiz.q: According to verses 10 to 11, when will God restore the exiles, and for what purpose?

options.text: After Babylon's seventy years are complete, according to His plans to give them a future and a hope.
options.note: Verse 10 sets the specific timeframe and verse 11 names God's purpose in restoring them.

options.text: Immediately, within the same year the letter was written.
options.note: The text explicitly sets a seventy-year timeframe, not an immediate return.

options.text: Only once every exile has personally repented in perfect detail.
options.note: The promise is tied to the seventy years, not an individualized repentance quota.

options.text: At an unspecified, open-ended future time with no set length.
options.note: The passage is unusually specific, naming seventy years, not leaving the timeframe open.

quiz.q: How should Jeremiah 29:11's plans to prosper you best be understood in context?

options.text: As God's promise to the whole exiled nation of a specific future restoration after seventy years, not a private guarantee of an easy life.
options.note: The you is plural and the promise is explicitly bound to the seventy years just named.

options.text: As an unconditional promise that any individual believer will avoid all hardship.
options.note: This reads the corporate, exile-specific promise as a private guarantee it was never meant to be.

options.text: As a promise only about financial wealth for the exiles.
options.note: The promise concerns national restoration and hope, not primarily financial gain.

options.text: As a vague sentiment with no real content or timeframe.
options.note: The promise is specific and dated, tied to the seventy years, not vague at all.

quiz.q: What judgment does God pronounce on Ahab and Zedekiah in verses 21 to 23?

options.text: They will be delivered to Nebuchadnezzar and killed, becoming a byword for a curse because they prophesied lies and committed adultery.
options.note: Verses 21 to 23 specify both their crimes and their fiery death before the exiles' eyes.

options.text: They will be honored as true prophets for their comforting message.
options.note: The opposite occurs, they are condemned and executed for false prophecy.

options.text: They will simply be exiled a second time to a different land.
options.note: Their judgment is death, not a second exile.

options.text: Nothing happens to them; the text leaves their fate unresolved.
options.note: Verses 21 to 22 explicitly describe their execution and the curse named after them.

quiz.q: What does the seventy-year promise of exile and restoration anticipate in the rest of Scripture?

options.text: Daniel reading this very promise and being moved to fervent prayer for his people's restoration, Daniel 9:2.
options.note: Daniel explicitly cites Jeremiah's seventy years as the basis for his prayer of repentance and hope.

options.text: No later biblical figure ever refers back to this promise.
options.note: Daniel 9:2 directly references this exact promise from Jeremiah.

options.text: A promise that is quietly dropped and never fulfilled anywhere in Scripture.
options.note: The exile's end is treated in Scripture as a real, historical fulfillment of this timeframe.

options.text: A promise fulfilled only in a symbolic, non-literal sense with no historical referent.
options.note: Scripture treats the seventy years and the return from exile as a real historical event, not merely symbolic.

questions: Jeremiah told the exiles to seek the welfare of the very city that held them captive. What would it look like for you to seek the good of a place or circumstance you did not choose and do not want to be in?
The exiles were tempted to trust popular, comforting prophets over God's actual, harder word. Where are you tempted to prefer a comfortable message over what God has actually said?

Jeremiah 29:11 is often quoted alone, out of its exile context. How does reading it as a promise to a whole community, tied to a specific waiting period, change how you hold your own hopes for the future?
God's restoration came only after seventy years of waiting, not immediately. Where is God calling you to faithful patience rather than an immediate escape from hard circumstances?
Shemaiah tried to silence Jeremiah's true word because it was unwelcome. Have you ever tried to silence or avoid a true but unwelcome word from God?

translationNotes.note: The same Hebrew word shalom translates prosperity, prospers, and prosper across this verse, the well-being of the exiles is deliberately tied to the well-being of the pagan city holding them.

Babylon's seventy years is widely reckoned from the first deportation in 605 BC to the decree of Cyrus permitting return around 538 BC, a datable, historical fulfillment rather than a symbolic round number alone.
The plural you throughout this letter addresses the whole community of exiles; the singular application often given to this verse today is a later, careful extension, not its first meaning.
